item.title: Static Motion by Carl Ander. Get it today from lleditions.se. - For over a decad...
item.description:
@lleditions

Static Motion by Carl Ander. Get it today from lleditions.se.
-
For over a decade, photographer Carl Ander has been obsessively collecting manuals illustrated with photographs on the subjects of sports, health, and self-help. Aspiring to teach everything from how to wrestle or ski to how to stretch or give a massage, these books encompass a broad range of topics and cultural contexts. Over time, Ander’s collection has grown to include hundreds of volumes published across the span of a century.

In the book Static Motion (LL’Editions, 2025), Ander presents a selection of photographs drawn from this collection—omitting their accompanying textual instructions. Removed from their original contexts, and with the logic underpinning their existence obscured, the viewer is invited to interpret these images anew. Through this act of recontextualization, Ander imbues the photographs with new layers of meaning. Detached from the utilitarian logic that once governed them, they invite new readings; at times absurd or humorous, at others unsettlingly violent or erotic.

Although their initial purpose—to instruct—has been lost, their capacity to teach is not entirely negated. Collectively, these images now seem to constitute a manual on how to photograph the body in motion. Frequently, the photographs are annotated with drawn arrows and other graphic interventions indicating direction or sequence of movement—visual cues that suggest a single frame can never fully capture motion. Likewise, when enlarged, the halftone rasters of the scanned images become pronounced, transforming into an aesthetic leitmotif that runs throughout the publication.

This selective encyclopedia of static motion marks the culmination of Ander’s sustained engagement with the photographic image as both document and fiction. The book is designed by the renowned design studio Lundgren+Lindqvist, who, for Static Motion, have reimagined the traditional form of the photobook.
-
Get it now (link in bio)
item.content:encoded:
@lleditions

Static Motion by Carl Ander. Get it today from lleditions.se.
-
For over a decade, photographer Carl Ander has been obsessively collecting manuals illustrated with photographs on the subjects of sports, health, and self-help. Aspiring to teach everything from how to wrestle or ski to how to stretch or give a massage, these books encompass a broad range of topics and cultural contexts. Over time, Ander’s collection has grown to include hundreds of volumes published across the span of a century.

In the book Static Motion (LL’Editions, 2025), Ander presents a selection of photographs drawn from this collection—omitting their accompanying textual instructions. Removed from their original contexts, and with the logic underpinning their existence obscured, the viewer is invited to interpret these images anew. Through this act of recontextualization, Ander imbues the photographs with new layers of meaning. Detached from the utilitarian logic that once governed them, they invite new readings; at times absurd or humorous, at others unsettlingly violent or erotic.

Although their initial purpose—to instruct—has been lost, their capacity to teach is not entirely negated. Collectively, these images now seem to constitute a manual on how to photograph the body in motion. Frequently, the photographs are annotated with drawn arrows and other graphic interventions indicating direction or sequence of movement—visual cues that suggest a single frame can never fully capture motion. Likewise, when enlarged, the halftone rasters of the scanned images become pronounced, transforming into an aesthetic leitmotif that runs throughout the publication.

This selective encyclopedia of static motion marks the culmination of Ander’s sustained engagement with the photographic image as both document and fiction. The book is designed by the renowned design studio Lundgren+Lindqvist, who, for Static Motion, have reimagined the traditional form of the photobook.
-
Get it now (link in bio)
item.author: lleditions

item.title: Photobook Feature: Static Motion by Carl Ander, published by @lleditions Drawing...
item.description:
@lleditions

Photobook Feature: Static Motion by Carl Ander, published by @lleditions

Drawing from his extensive collection of 20th-century sports, health, and self-help manuals, @carl.ander_ recontextualises over 300 instructional photographs, removing their original purpose and inviting new, often unexpected readings of the body in motion.

You can discover Carl’s book in our photobook-dedicated section: phmuseum.com/photobooks

#phmuseum #photobook #lleditions
item.content:encoded:
@lleditions

Photobook Feature: Static Motion by Carl Ander, published by @lleditions

Drawing from his extensive collection of 20th-century sports, health, and self-help manuals, @carl.ander_ recontextualises over 300 instructional photographs, removing their original purpose and inviting new, often unexpected readings of the body in motion.

You can discover Carl’s book in our photobook-dedicated section: phmuseum.com/photobooks

#phmuseum #photobook #lleditions
item.author: lleditions

item.title: Excerpt from ’Tal R:’ (47:42). Watch the full video in our cinema section (link ...
item.description:
@lleditions

Excerpt from ’Tal R:’ (47:42).
Watch the full video in our cinema section (link in bio).
-
“There’s one character, in the world of characters, that I like the most, and that character is the colon.” Tal R uses the colon to illustrate the relationship between the past, present and future. In this film, Tal R – on the brink of turning fifty – looks forwards as well as backwards and shares what being an artist and a human being means to him, and why the two can’t be separated.
-
LL’Editions Cinema includes a stream of new and old videos that inspire us. We’ve collected artist’s talks, studio visits, documentaries and interviews with artists, architects and designers and have over 200 videos.
-
Watch the full video and many more at lleditions.se/cinema (link in bio).
item.content:encoded:
@lleditions

Excerpt from ’Tal R:’ (47:42).
Watch the full video in our cinema section (link in bio).
-
“There’s one character, in the world of characters, that I like the most, and that character is the colon.” Tal R uses the colon to illustrate the relationship between the past, present and future. In this film, Tal R – on the brink of turning fifty – looks forwards as well as backwards and shares what being an artist and a human being means to him, and why the two can’t be separated.
-
LL’Editions Cinema includes a stream of new and old videos that inspire us. We’ve collected artist’s talks, studio visits, documentaries and interviews with artists, architects and designers and have over 200 videos.
-
Watch the full video and many more at lleditions.se/cinema (link in bio).
item.author: lleditions
